steps.title: Choose your Subscription
steps.explanation: A VM Scale Set (VMSS) bills per VM instance that runs. With autoscaling, costs vary dynamically — set budget alerts on the subscription to avoid surprises.

steps.title: Select or create a Resource Group
steps.explanation: Place the Scale Set in the same Resource Group as the load balancer and VNet it will use. This keeps the entire application stack manageable as a unit.

steps.title: Give the Scale Set a name
steps.explanation: Each VM instance in the set gets a name based on this prefix, e.g. 'webscaleset000000', 'webscaleset000001'. Keep the name short to leave room for the instance suffix.

steps.title: Choose a region
steps.explanation: Choose the same region as your load balancer and backend services. For higher availability, use Availability Zones — this spreads instances across physically separate datacenters within the region.

steps.title: Choose the Orchestration mode
steps.explanation: Flexible: each VM is a full Azure VM with its own NIC — best for most workloads and supports mixing VM sizes. Uniform: identical VMs managed as a group — suited for stateless, high-scale scenarios like web frontends.

steps.title: Choose an Image
steps.explanation: All VMs in the Scale Set use the same base image. Choose a consistent image that includes your application or use Azure VM Extensions / cloud-init to configure VMs on startup.

steps.title: Configure scaling
steps.explanation: Manual: you control the instance count. Autoscaling: Azure adds or removes instances based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Set a minimum and maximum to control costs and ensure availability.

steps.title: Click Review + create
steps.explanation: After deployment, attach a Load Balancer or Application Gateway to distribute traffic across instances. Configure the health probe so Azure automatically replaces unhealthy instances.
